title: [release-1.44] cherry-pick some fixes and a feature, then tag 1.44.1
body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug
/kind documentation
/kind feature

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Cherry picks these changes:
* Commit: add an EmptyLayerIfEmptyDiff flag (#6873)
* build: commit an empty layer instead of a layer with nothing in it (#6873)
* imagebuildah: default RUN --mount type to bind for cache checksums (#6957)
* parse: honor containers.conf seccomp_profile (#6908)
* internal/mkcw.GenerateMeasurement(): just glob for libkrunfw-sev.so.* (#6934)
* seccomp: fix --security-opt seccomp=profile silently ignored (#6960)
* Add support for symbolic chmod options (#6778)
* build: accept "FROM containers-storage:" prefixes again (#6982)

Then tags a v1.44.1 release.

#### How to verify it

New changes came with new tests!

#### Which issue(s) this PR fixes:

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
This release includes bug fixes and a couple of feature changes from the main branch:
* Commit: add an EmptyLayerIfEmptyDiff flag (#6873)
* build: commit an empty layer instead of a layer with nothing in it (#6873)
* imagebuildah: default RUN --mount type to bind for cache checksums (#6957)
* parse: honor containers.conf seccomp_profile (#6908)
* internal/mkcw.GenerateMeasurement(): just glob for libkrunfw-sev.so.* (#6934)
* seccomp: fix --security-opt seccomp=profile silently ignored (#6960)
* Add support for symbolic chmod options (#6778)
* build: accept "FROM containers-storage:" prefixes again (#6982)
```

comments.body: Ephemeral COPR build failed. @containers/packit-build please check.

> Add support for symbolic chmod options

That is kinda a feature? Patch releases are for bug fixes only IMO. I don't feel strongly about this particular case but in general I like to avoid features in patch releases.

LGTM
some tests look to be flaking, restarted. 
I'm OK with the changes given it's bumping the version, fwiw.

>  I'm OK with the changes given it's bumping the version, fwiw.

Well that is my point, we use semantic versioning so IMHO we should follow the principle there https://semver.org/
bumping a patch version implies no new features, just fixes

> Given a version number MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH, increment the:

    MAJOR version when you make incompatible API changes
    MINOR version when you add functionality in a backward compatible manner
    PATCH version when you make backward compatible bug fixes


Anyhow given we never followed that consistently I don't care that much here as I am not the main maintainer.
